purpose: 10/10 report enhancements - additional context, insights, and domain knowledge

files.description: Adds the 'tip of the iceberg' framing: $12.5B reported but FTC estimates $100B+ actual
Names and explains the dominant scam technique hiding in Investment/Imposter categories
files.display: expandable explainer box within fraud types section

files.description: Highlights surprising per-capita findings: DC at 2x California, Texas drops from #2 to #33
files.display: two-column comparison map (raw vs per capita)

files.description: Explains what the data means for fraud teams: monitoring gap, upstream detection
files.display: expert commentary block at end of loss section or in implications

files.description: Two forward-looking statements: Credit Bureau crossover, social media regulation
files.display: forecast callout at end of trends section

integration_notes.executive_summary: Lead with unreported_multiplier callout to establish stakes before showing stats
integration_notes.fraud_types: Include pig_butchering_explainer as expandable callout near Investment/Imposter categories
integration_notes.geography: Use per_capita_insights to reframe the section around surprises, not obvious state rankings
integration_notes.loss_payments: Reference domain_perspective at section end for practitioner context
integration_notes.trends: End with forward_outlook projections to make report forward-looking, not just backward-looking

headline_rewrites.note: Use these tension-based headlines instead of stat-based headlines

rewrites.rewrite: Social media converts 7x more fraud contacts into losses than email
Bank transfers: 10% of reports, 38% of losses
Seniors don't fall for more scams. They fall for bigger ones.
Credit Bureau complaints grew 107pp above market. This isn't fraud. It's the cleanup.
Fraud has grown faster than GDP, population, or internet adoption for 24 years

methodology_additions: FTC Sentinel data is voluntary. It captures reported fraud, not actual fraud.
FTC estimates suggest actual losses may be 15-20x higher than reported figures.
The data cannot link fraud categories to payment methods at the individual report level, limiting cross-dimensional analysis.
Credit Bureau complaints are not fraud events themselves but downstream effects of identity theft.
13.88% CAGR compares to 2.5% US population growth and 3.2% GDP growth over the same period.

glossary_additions.definition: A long-con scam where fraudsters build relationships over weeks before convincing victims to invest in fake crypto platforms. Losses average $50K+ per victim.
Fraud where scammers impersonate trusted entities (government, businesses, family members) to extract money or information.
Consumer complaints about incorrect information on credit reports. Often downstream effects of identity theft, not fraud events themselves.
The percentage of fraud contacts that result in actual financial loss. Higher conversion rates indicate more effective scam techniques.
